paragraphs.paragraph_text: CARCINOMA BREAST CARCINOMA BREAST Case definition: Suspect case of Ca Breast (For Level 1, 2, 3 and 4 facility) It is based on clinical grounds. A female patient having a firm or hard lump in the breast with or without axillary lymph nodes, nipple discharge or ulceration over the breast is likely to have Ca breast. Advanced cases may present with symptoms suggestive of bone, brain, liver or lung metastasis. The patient may also have a family history of Ca Breastin the first degree relatives. The group of patients with the following risk factors has a greater chance of developing breast carcinoma. A. Major Risk Factors: e Female sex: Ca Breastis very uncommon in the male breast (< 1%). e Age: Ca Breast is very uncommon before 20y, uncommon before 30y and then the incidence rises. Chances of developing Ca Breast in the 6th decade of life are 1 in 24 as compared to 1 in 257 in the 3rd decade. e Personal history of contralateral Ca Breast. e Family history of breast cancer especially in first degree relatives (mother, sister, daughter) more so if they had the cancer when premenopausal or had bilateral cancer. e Non•invasive cancer (ductal or lobular Ca•in•situ). e Benign proliferative changes with atypia. B. Minor Risk Factors: e Earlymenarche e Late menopause e Nulliparity or first child after age 31 years e Obesity e Lowdose radiation e UseofHRT and OC pills e Alcohol Adetailed physical examination should then be performed in the following manner: Breast examination should be done in a well lit room with respect to patient's privacy and comfort. Patient should be examined in lying, sitting and bending forward position. Look for: e Asymmetry and skin changes in the two breasts. e Obvious mass and its characteristics including fixity to skin and underlying structures. e Nipple discharge, retraction, excoriation or inversion e Axillary lymph nodes: number, location and fixity e LtSupraclavicular lymph node e Evidence of metastasis: hepatomegaly, bony tenderness, lung metastasis Probable case of Ca Breast (For Level 3 & 4 facility) e Suspicion and Clinical Examination should be as above. e AtLevel 3 facilityan FNAC should be performed from the breast lump to confirm the diagnosis. STANDARD TREATMENT GUIDELINES 135
sentences: CARCINOMA BREAST CARCINOMA BREAST Case definition: Suspect case of Ca Breast (For Level 1, 2, 3 and 4 facility) It is based on clinical grounds.
A female patient having a firm or hard lump in the breast with or without axillary lymph nodes, nipple discharge or ulceration over the breast is likely to have Ca breast.
Advanced cases may present with symptoms suggestive of bone, brain, liver or lung metastasis.
The patient may also have a family history of Ca Breastin the first degree relatives.
The group of patients with the following risk factors has a greater chance of developing breast carcinoma.
A.
Major Risk Factors: e Female sex: Ca Breastis very uncommon in the male breast (< 1%).
e Age: Ca Breast is very uncommon before 20y, uncommon before 30y and then the incidence rises.
Chances of developing Ca Breast in the 6th decade of life are 1 in 24 as compared to 1 in 257 in the 3rd decade.
e Personal history of contralateral Ca Breast.
e Family history of breast cancer especially in first degree relatives (mother, sister, daughter) more so if they had the cancer when premenopausal or had bilateral cancer.
e Non•invasive cancer (ductal or lobular Ca•in•situ).
e Benign proliferative changes with atypia.
B.

Minor Risk Factors: e Earlymenarche e Late menopause e Nulliparity or first child after age 31 years e Obesity e Lowdose radiation e UseofHRT and OC pills e Alcohol Adetailed physical examination should then be performed in the following manner: Breast examination should be done in a well lit room with respect to patient's privacy and comfort.
Patient should be examined in lying, sitting and bending forward position.
Look for: e Asymmetry and skin changes in the two breasts.
e Obvious mass and its characteristics including fixity to skin and underlying structures.

e Nipple discharge, retraction, excoriation or inversion e Axillary lymph nodes: number, location and fixity e LtSupraclavicular lymph node e Evidence of metastasis: hepatomegaly, bony tenderness, lung metastasis Probable case of Ca Breast (For Level 3 & 4 facility) e Suspicion and Clinical Examination should be as above.
e AtLevel 3 facilityan FNAC should be performed from the breast lump to confirm the diagnosis.
STANDARD TREATMENT GUIDELINES 135

paragraphs.paragraph_text: CARCINOMA BREAST o FNAC maybe USG guided. o If facility is not available, the same may be requisitioned from a nearby centre where it is available or it may be got done at Level 4. e Forsuspicious lumps, USG and mammography is required. e If planned, they should be done prior to FNAC. e Incase of negative FNAC but where the clinical suspicion is high, a Trucut or an open biopsy should be resorted to. e [tis made sure that the biopsy incision is such that it can be included in the subsequent surgery. e Estrogen Receptor (ER)/ Progesterone Receptor (PR) status should also be known at this time from the FNAC/ Trucut biopsy. e Once the diagnosis is confirmed, the disease is staged. e Chest X•Ray and USG of the abdomen are done to look for metastatic disease. © Incase symptomatic with bone pain, an X•ray of the particular bone is done. co A bone scan is routinely performed to look for bony metastasis in case of locally advanced / advanced disease. e The patientis staged as per the UICC TNM classification which is given below (Table 1). e Depending on the stage of the disease, the patient is offered formal surgery in the form of Modified Radical Mastectomy (MRM) or Wide Local Excision (WLE) followed by Chemotherapy (CT), Radiotherapy (RT) and/or Hormonal Therapy (HT) neo•adjuvant CT followed by surgery or only palliative therapy. e AtLevel 3 facility, surgery can be performed and the patient referred to Level 4 for further management. At Level 4 facility complete comprehensive treatment can be given. NCE OF THE CONDITION IN OUR COUNTRY The exact incidence of Breast Cancer in our country is not known. However it is the second commonest malignancy in females after Carcinoma of the Cervix and the commonest malignancy in females in the West. It is common in age group above 40 years. The overall 5 year survival in developing countries is between 30•50% due to late detection, late reporting, non availability of adequate and appropriate medication and absence of screening programmes. DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS e Benign Breast Disease e Traumatic fat necrosis of the breast e Tuberculosis of the Breast IA, INVESTIGATIONS, TREATMENT & REFERRAL CRITERIA LEVEL 1: AT SOLO PHYSICIAN CLINIC & LEVEL 2: AT 6•10 BEDDED PRIMARY HEALTH CENTRE Clinical Diagnosis: The diagnosis of Breast Cancer cannot be confirmed at this level. However a strong clinical suspicion can be made with the help of a good history and sound clinical examination. Patient having a firm or hard lump in the breast with or without axillary lymph nodes or nipple discharge. The patient may present with symptoms suggestive of bone, brain, liver or lung metastasis. The patient may also have a family history of Ca Breast in the first degree relatives or a history of surgery in the other breast. 136 STANDARD TREATMENT GUIDELINES
sentences: CARCINOMA BREAST o FNAC maybe USG guided.
o If facility is not available, the same may be requisitioned from a nearby centre where it is available or it may be got done at Level 4. e Forsuspicious lumps, USG and mammography is required.
e If planned, they should be done prior to FNAC.
e Incase of negative FNAC but where the clinical suspicion is high, a Trucut or an open biopsy should be resorted to.
e [tis made sure that the biopsy incision is such that it can be included in the subsequent surgery.
e Estrogen Receptor (ER)/ Progesterone Receptor (PR) status should also be known at this time from the FNAC/ Trucut biopsy.
e Once the diagnosis is confirmed, the disease is staged.
e Chest X•Ray and USG of the abdomen are done to look for metastatic disease.
© Incase symptomatic with bone pain, an X•ray of the particular bone is done.
co A bone scan is routinely performed to look for bony metastasis in case of locally advanced / advanced disease.
e The patientis staged as per the UICC TNM classification which is given below (Table 1).

e Depending on the stage of the disease, the patient is offered formal surgery in the form of Modified Radical Mastectomy (MRM) or Wide Local Excision (WLE) followed by Chemotherapy (CT), Radiotherapy (RT) and/or Hormonal Therapy (HT) neo•adjuvant CT followed by surgery or only palliative therapy.
e AtLevel 3 facility, surgery can be performed and the patient referred to Level 4 for further management.
At Level 4 facility complete comprehensive treatment can be given.
NCE OF THE CONDITION IN OUR COUNTRY The exact incidence of Breast Cancer in our country is not known.
However it is the second commonest malignancy in females after Carcinoma of the Cervix and the commonest malignancy in females in the West.
It is common in age group above 40 years.

The overall 5 year survival in developing countries is between 30•50% due to late detection, late reporting, non availability of adequate and appropriate medication and absence of screening programmes.

DIFFERENTIAL DIAGNOSIS e Benign Breast Disease e Traumatic fat necrosis of the breast e Tuberculosis of the Breast IA, INVESTIGATIONS, TREATMENT & REFERRAL CRITERIA LEVEL 1: AT SOLO PHYSICIAN CLINIC & LEVEL 2: AT 6•10 BEDDED PRIMARY HEALTH CENTRE Clinical Diagnosis: The diagnosis of Breast Cancer cannot be confirmed at this level.
However a strong clinical suspicion can be made with the help of a good history and sound clinical examination.
Patient having a firm or hard lump in the breast with or without axillary lymph nodes or nipple discharge.
The patient may present with symptoms suggestive of bone, brain, liver or lung metastasis.
The patient may also have a family history of Ca Breast in the first degree relatives or a history of surgery in the other breast.
136 STANDARD TREATMENT GUIDELINES

paragraphs.paragraph_text: CARCINOMA BREAST Investigations: The patient should not be investigated but explained the likely possibility and forwarded to the next level. Treatment: No treatment needs to be offered at this level. Only dressings need be done if the patient has presented at the stage of a fungating mass lesion. Referral criteria: Suspicion of Carcinoma Breast based on history and clinical examination. The patient is forwarded with a case sheet containing history and clinical findings in detail. LEVEL 3: AT 30•100 BEDDED COMMUNITY HEALTH CENTRE Clinical Diagnosis: Same as Level 1 & 2 fora fresh case reporting directly. Investigations: 1. To Confirm Diagnosis: FNAC. This is done from the main lump to confirm diagnosis and to ascertain ER / PR status. FNAC may be USG guided. In case FNAC is inconclusive, a 'tru•cut' or ‘Open biopsy' may be required to take out more representative tissue for pathological examination. This can be done at a nearby centre where facilities are available or the patient may be referred to Level 4 for the same. 2. To Stage the Disease: Ultrasound Abdomen. To rule out liver metastasis X Ray Chest. To rule out lung metastasis X Rays of particular bone sites depending upon patient symptomatology. Bone Scan may be done in cases of locally advanced / advanced disease to look for bone metastasis. It is also done if the X•Ray shows a suspicious area or if the alkaline phosphatase is raised. 3. To Work up for Surgery: Blood. Hb, TLC, DLC, BT, CT, ABORh, urea, creatinine, sugar, LFT withAST, ALT and Alkaline Phosphatase Urine RE. ECG. Treatment: Early Breast Cancer (EBC) Stage I, Il: Patients are offered surgery in the form of Modified Radical Mastectomy (MRM) or Wide Local Excision (WLE) with axillary node sampling. Subsequently based on the pathology report, any patient with lesion more than 1 cm or more than 0.5 cm with unfavourable characteristics like lymphovascular invasion should undergo Chemotherapy (CT) (Chemotherapy Schedule Table 2). Apatient with node positivity irrespective of the size of the tumor should undergo CT. Radiotherapy (RT) (RT schedule Table 3) is given if WLE is done or if the margin of resection is positive. Hormonal therapy (HT) in the form of Tamoxifen 20 mg OD for the next 5 years in case of postmenopausal and ER/ PR +ve cases. For CT and RT patient has to be referred to Level 4. Referral criteria: Need for Chemotherapy/ Radiotherapy. Most patients in our setting come at a stage with tumors warranting either neoadjuvant or adjuvant chemotherapy. These should be referred to Level 4 either after surgery (Early Breast Cancer) or before (Locally Advanced Breast Cancer). This should always be done with the blocks and slides along with a detailed history and clinical notes. LEVEL 4: AT 100 OR MORE BEDDED DISTRICT HOSPITAL Clinical Diagnosis: Same as Level 3 for a fresh case reporting directly. STANDARD TREATMEN IDELINES

Treatment: No treatment needs to be offered at this level.
Only dressings need be done if the patient has presented at the stage of a fungating mass lesion.
Referral criteria: Suspicion of Carcinoma Breast based on history and clinical examination.
The patient is forwarded with a case sheet containing history and clinical findings in detail.
LEVEL 3: AT 30•100 BEDDED COMMUNITY HEALTH CENTRE Clinical Diagnosis: Same as Level 1 & 2 fora fresh case reporting directly.
Investigations: 1.
To Confirm Diagnosis: FNAC.
This is done from the main lump to confirm diagnosis and to ascertain ER / PR status.
FNAC may be USG guided.
In case FNAC is inconclusive, a 'tru•cut' or ‘Open biopsy' may be required to take out more representative tissue for pathological examination.
This can be done at a nearby centre where facilities are available or the patient may be referred to Level 4 for the same.
To Stage the Disease: Ultrasound Abdomen.
To rule out liver metastasis X Ray Chest.
To rule out lung metastasis X Rays of particular bone sites depending upon patient symptomatology.
Bone Scan may be done in cases of locally advanced / advanced disease to look for bone metastasis.
It is also done if the X•Ray shows a suspicious area or if the alkaline phosphatase is raised.
To Work up for Surgery: Blood.
Hb, TLC, DLC, BT, CT, ABORh, urea, creatinine, sugar, LFT withAST, ALT and Alkaline Phosphatase Urine RE.
ECG.
Treatment: Early Breast Cancer (EBC) Stage I, Il: Patients are offered surgery in the form of Modified Radical Mastectomy (MRM) or Wide Local Excision (WLE) with axillary node sampling.

Subsequently based on the pathology report, any patient with lesion more than 1 cm or more than 0.5 cm with unfavourable characteristics like lymphovascular invasion should undergo Chemotherapy (CT) (Chemotherapy Schedule Table 2).
Apatient with node positivity irrespective of the size of the tumor should undergo CT. Radiotherapy (RT) (RT schedule Table 3) is given if WLE is done or if the margin of resection is positive.
Hormonal therapy (HT) in the form of Tamoxifen 20 mg OD for the next 5 years in case of postmenopausal and ER/ PR +ve cases.
For CT and RT patient has to be referred to Level 4.
Referral criteria: Need for Chemotherapy/ Radiotherapy.
Most patients in our setting come at a stage with tumors warranting either neoadjuvant or adjuvant chemotherapy.
These should be referred to Level 4 either after surgery (Early Breast Cancer) or before (Locally Advanced Breast Cancer).
This should always be done with the blocks and slides along with a detailed history and clinical notes.
LEVEL 4: AT 100 OR MORE BEDDED DISTRICT HOSPITAL Clinical Diagnosis: Same as Level 3 for a fresh case reporting directly.
STANDARD TREATMEN IDELINES

paragraphs.paragraph_text: CARCINOMA BREAST Investigations: Same as Level 3 for a fresh case reporting directly. Confirmation of diagnosis with the blocks and slides in case patient comes with FNAC/ Biopsy/ Surgery. 1. To Confirm Diagnosis: FNAC. This is done from the main lump to confirm diagnosis and to ascertain ER / PR status. FNAC may be USG guided. In case FNAC is inconclusive, a tru•cut or Open biopsy may be required to take out more representative tissue for pathological examination. 2. To Stage the Disease: Ultrasound Abdomen. To rule out liver metastasis X Ray Chest. To rule out lung metastasis X Rays of particular bone sites depending upon patient symptomatology. Bone Scan may be done in cases of locally advanced / advanced disease to look for bone metastasis. It is also done if the X•Ray shows a suspicious area. 3. To Work up for Surgery: Blood. Hb, TLC, DLC, BT, CT, ABORh, urea, creatinine, sugar Urine RE. ECG. Table 1: UICC TNM Classification for Carcinoma Breast (Includes assessment by imaging) T (Primary Tumor) TX — Primary tumour cannot be assessed TO Noevidence of primary tumour Tis | Carcinomain situ or Paget's disease of the nipple with no tumour. T1 2cmorlessin greatest dimension T2 Tumourmore than 2 cm but not more than 5 cm in greatest dimension T3 Tumourmore than 5cm in greatest dimension T4 Tumour ofany size with direct extension to chest wall or skin T4a Extension to chest wall T4b Oedema (including peau d orange) or ulceration of the skin of breast or satellite nodules confined to same breast T4c Both T4a and T4b T4d Inflammatory carcinoma (N) Regional lymph nodes 138 NX_ Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed (e.g., previously removed) NO N1 N2 N3 No regional lymph node metastasis Metastasis to movable ipsilateral axillary lymph node(s) N2a Metastasis to ipsilateral axillary nodes fixed to one another (matted) or to other structures N2b Metastasis on imaging studies to ipsilateral internal mammary nodes N3a Metastasis to ipsilateral infraclavicular lymph nodes N3b Metastasis in ipsilateral internal mammary and axillary lymph node(s) N3c Metastasis in ipsilateral Supraclavicular lymph nodes STANDARD TREATMENT GUIDELINES

Confirmation of diagnosis with the blocks and slides in case patient comes with FNAC/ Biopsy/ Surgery.
To Confirm Diagnosis: FNAC.
This is done from the main lump to confirm diagnosis and to ascertain ER / PR status.
FNAC may be USG guided.
In case FNAC is inconclusive, a tru•cut or Open biopsy may be required to take out more representative tissue for pathological examination.
To Stage the Disease: Ultrasound Abdomen.
To rule out liver metastasis X Ray Chest.
To rule out lung metastasis X Rays of particular bone sites depending upon patient symptomatology.
Bone Scan may be done in cases of locally advanced / advanced disease to look for bone metastasis.
It is also done if the X•Ray shows a suspicious area.
To Work up for Surgery: Blood.
Hb, TLC, DLC, BT, CT, ABORh, urea, creatinine, sugar Urine RE.
ECG.

Table 1: UICC TNM Classification for Carcinoma Breast (Includes assessment by imaging) T (Primary Tumor) TX — Primary tumour cannot be assessed TO Noevidence of primary tumour Tis | Carcinomain situ or Paget's disease of the nipple with no tumour.

T1 2cmorlessin greatest dimension T2 Tumourmore than 2 cm but not more than 5 cm in greatest dimension T3 Tumourmore than 5cm in greatest dimension T4 Tumour ofany size with direct extension to chest wall or skin T4a Extension to chest wall T4b Oedema (including peau d orange) or ulceration of the skin of breast or satellite nodules confined to same breast T4c Both T4a and T4b T4d Inflammatory carcinoma (N) Regional lymph nodes 138 NX_ Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed (e.g., previously removed) NO N1 N2 N3 No regional lymph node metastasis Metastasis to movable ipsilateral axillary lymph node(s) N2a Metastasis to ipsilateral axillary nodes fixed to one another (matted) or to other structures N2b Metastasis on imaging studies to ipsilateral internal mammary nodes N3a Metastasis to ipsilateral infraclavicular lymph nodes N3b Metastasis in ipsilateral internal mammary and axillary lymph node(s) N3c Metastasis in ipsilateral Supraclavicular lymph nodes STANDARD TREATMENT GUIDELINES

paragraphs.paragraph_text: CARCINOMA BREAST Distant metastasis (M) MX __ Distant metastasis cannot be assessed MO Nodistant metastasis M1__ Distant metastasis Stage grouping (AJCC Jan 2003) Stage 0 Tis NO MO Stage | 71 NO Mo Stage IIA TO N1 MO T1 N1 MO T2 NO Mo Stage IIB T2 N1 Mo T3 NO Mo Stage IIIA TO N2 Mo T1 N2 Mo T2 N2 Mo T3 N1 MO T3 N2 Mo Stage IIIB T4 NO Mo T4 N1 Mo T4 N2 Mo Stage IIIC AnyT N3 MO Stage IV AnyT AnyN M1 Treatment: 1. Early Breast Cancer (EBC) Stage I, Il: Patients are offered surgery in the form of Modified Radical Mastectomy (MRM) or Wide Local Excision (WLE) with axillary node sampling. Subsequently based on the pathology report, any patient with lesion more than 1 cm or more than 0.5 cm with unfavourable characteristics like lymphovascular invasion should undergo Chemotherapy (CT) (Chemotherapy Schedule Table 2). A patient with node positivity irrespective of the size of the tumor should undergo CT. Radiotherapy (RT) (RT schedule Table 3) is given if WLE is done or if the margin of resection is positive. Hormonal therapy (HT) in the form of Tamoxifen 20 mg OD for the next 5 years in case of postmenopausal and ER/ PR +ve cases. 2. Locally Advanced Breast Cancer (LABC) Stage III: Patients are offered Neoadjuvant or anterior CT as per the following protocol. The response is assessed after each cycle. After 4 cycles, if the lesion has shown a favourable response and significant regression, surgery (MRM) is done. Paclitaxel based CT is the given postoperatively. Patients who show 'No Response' or 'Disease Progression’ i.e. (increase in size while on CT) are given trial CT regimes. HT is given as for patients with EBC. 3. Advanced Breast Cancer Stage IV: HT is given as for patients with EBC. Toilet (Simple) mastectomy is offered for patients with fungating lesions. Experimental CT protocols can be tried in these patients STANDARD TREATMENT GUIDELINES 139
sentences: CARCINOMA BREAST Distant metastasis (M) MX __ Distant metastasis cannot be assessed MO Nodistant metastasis M1__ Distant metastasis Stage grouping (AJCC Jan 2003) Stage 0 Tis NO MO Stage | 71 NO Mo Stage IIA TO N1 MO T1 N1 MO T2 NO Mo Stage IIB T2 N1 Mo T3 NO Mo Stage IIIA TO N2 Mo T1 N2 Mo T2 N2 Mo T3 N1 MO T3 N2 Mo Stage IIIB T4 NO Mo T4 N1 Mo T4 N2 Mo Stage IIIC AnyT N3 MO Stage IV AnyT AnyN M1 Treatment: 1.
Early Breast Cancer (EBC) Stage I, Il: Patients are offered surgery in the form of Modified Radical Mastectomy (MRM) or Wide Local Excision (WLE) with axillary node sampling.

Subsequently based on the pathology report, any patient with lesion more than 1 cm or more than 0.5 cm with unfavourable characteristics like lymphovascular invasion should undergo Chemotherapy (CT) (Chemotherapy Schedule Table 2).
A patient with node positivity irrespective of the size of the tumor should undergo CT. Radiotherapy (RT) (RT schedule Table 3) is given if WLE is done or if the margin of resection is positive.
Hormonal therapy (HT) in the form of Tamoxifen 20 mg OD for the next 5 years in case of postmenopausal and ER/ PR +ve cases.
Locally Advanced Breast Cancer (LABC) Stage III: Patients are offered Neoadjuvant or anterior CT as per the following protocol.
The response is assessed after each cycle.
After 4 cycles, if the lesion has shown a favourable response and significant regression, surgery (MRM) is done.
Paclitaxel based CT is the given postoperatively.
Patients who show 'No Response' or 'Disease Progression’ i.e.
(increase in size while on CT) are given trial CT regimes.
HT is given as for patients with EBC.
Advanced Breast Cancer Stage IV: HT is given as for patients with EBC.
Toilet (Simple) mastectomy is offered for patients with fungating lesions.
Experimental CT protocols can be tried in these patients STANDARD TREATMENT GUIDELINES 139

paragraphs.paragraph_text: CARCINOMA BREAST Table 2 (Chemotherapy Schedule) 1. CEF (For EBC): e Inj Cyclophosphamide 500mg/m’* IV ¢ InjEpirubicin 75mg/m° IV e Inj5FU 500 mg/m’ IV Repeat every 28 days for 6 cycles 2. CAF (For LABC): e InjCyclophosphamide 500mg/m’ IVDay 1 e InjAdriamycin 50mg/m’ —s—‘|VDay 1 e Inj5FU 500mg/m* |VDay 1&8 Repeat every 28 days for 4 cycles followed by surgery followed by e Inj Paclitaxel 175mg/m’ IV Day 1 (2 cycles every 28 days) Table 3 (Radiotherapy Schedule): 6000 Gy divided between breast, axilla and supraclavicular region. (4500 Gy to Breast if margin positive and after breast conservative energy and 1500 Gy to the supraclavicular region) SUGGESTED READING 1. Sabiston Text Book of Surgery, edited by CM Townsend Jr, RD Beauchamp, B Mark Evers, KL Mattox; 17th edition. 2005, published by Saunders, Elsevier, Philadelphia, USA. ISBN•13:978•81•8147•950•1. 2.
